subsection_title: Unintended prefix advertisement from VRF into EVPN fabric
text: EVPN type-5 routes carry IP prefixes from a VRF into the EVPN fabric. A type-5 route count spike typically indicates unintended prefix advertisement — either a VRF import/export policy is too permissive, a connected route is being leaked without the correct route-target filtering, or a BGP aggregate is being redistributed into EVPN. On Arista EOS, type-5 routes are visible via 'show bgp evpn route-type ip-prefix'. The route-target attached to each type-5 route determines which VRFs import it — an incorrect RT can cause prefixes to leak into VRFs that should not receive them. Diagnosis requires: (1) identifying the originating device via the next-hop and RD, (2) checking the VRF route-map and import/export targets on the originating device, (3) verifying that only intended prefixes are being redistributed into BGP EVPN.

section_title: EVPN Type-5 Route Leaking on NX-OS
subsection_title: VRF route-target filtering and prefix advertisement control
text: On Cisco NX-OS, type-5 EVPN route leaking is controlled by the route-target import and export statements in the VRF address-family configuration. A common misconfiguration is using wildcard or overly broad route-targets that cause prefixes from one VRF to be imported into another. The 'show bgp l2vpn evpn route-type 5' command lists all type-5 routes with their RD and RT values. To identify leaking, compare the RT on the received type-5 routes against the import RT configured on each VRF. The 'show ip route vrf' command confirms which type-5 prefixes have been installed. Clearing type-5 route advertisements requires a soft reset of the BGP EVPN session — this is a disruptive operation that should be approved before execution.

section_title: EVPN Type-5 Route Leaking on Junos
subsection_title: Routing instance export policy and type-5 prefix control
text: On Juniper platforms, EVPN type-5 routes originate from routing instances configured with vrf-target and vrf-import/vrf-export policies. Type-5 route leaking occurs when the vrf-export policy matches routes that should remain contained within the originating VRF. The 'show route table bgp.evpn.0' command lists all EVPN routes including type-5 prefix routes with their route-distinguisher. The 'show route advertising-protocol bgp' command identifies which prefixes are being advertised to each BGP peer. To isolate a leak, compare the prefixes advertised by the suspected origin device against the intended routing policy. Policy changes affecting type-5 advertisement require careful validation — use 'test policy' before committing changes to the export policy.
